title: ‘Booksmart’ Bombed. Why Couldn't the Internet Save It? Plus, the Oscar Favorites at the Cannes Film Festival
format: A grab-bag episode: why Booksmart tanked against Aladdin over Memorial Day, what Cannes signaled about the 2019-20 Oscar race, the year's crop of political documentaries, and Joanna Hogg's The Souvenir.

segments.heading: The 'Booksmart' box-office post-mortem
segments.note: and the internet hype cycle it set off

films.title: Booksmart
films.note: Olivia Wilde's teen comedy, opened wide on 2,000 screens over Memorial Day and made a fraction of what the hosts hoped. They pin the flop on distribution — a bad wide-release call by Annapurna/MGM — not on feminism or cinema dying, and flag the film's blind spot: it's about rich West Side kids with no class anxiety.

films.title: Aladdin
films.note: The live-action Will Smith genie remake that outgrossed Booksmart by roughly 20x the same weekend. The hosts call the two a false binary — a magazine versus a novel.

films.title: Superbad
films.note: The comp Booksmart chased, down to the same narrative shape. Sean's verdict: two clear, direct words for a title, and a dark core Booksmart lacks — plus we're past the Apatow studio-comedy moment.

films.title: Lady Bird
films.note: Sean's model for how it should have gone: a 100% score blown up by one critic, but platformed slowly from a handful of theaters to build word of mouth, not dumped wide.

films.title: Detroit
films.note: Annapurna's only prior wide release, Kathryn Bigelow's follow-up to Zero Dark Thirty — it didn't work, evidence the company doesn't know how to do wide.

films.title: The Breakfast Club
films.note: Held up as the teen movie that was conscious of class and pecking order, the thing Booksmart leaves out.

films.title: Zero Dark Thirty
films.note: Bigelow's film that Detroit followed.

films.title: The Master
films.note: The Annapurna prestige side — great if you're a fan, less so if you're a theater owner counting returns.

films.title: Long Shot
films.note: Another small, well-liked movie with young stars swallowed by the big release calendar — same conversation, different week.

films.title: Girls Trip
films.note: Cited to rebut the 'nothing since Bridesmaids' narrative — a Universal release that worked because Universal knows how to release movies.

films.title: Bridesmaids
films.note: The perpetual benchmark dragged out whenever a female-led comedy needs a savior narrative.

films.title: Crazy Rich Asians
films.note: Example of consumer-choice-as-identity marketing done successfully.

films.title: Black Panther
films.note: Another film that leaned into 'your ticket is a statement.'

films.title: Green Book
films.note: Invoked for the 'people want stories that end happily' thesis.

films.title: Ghostbusters
films.note: Recalled for the troll controversy around it.

films.title: John Wick: Chapter 3 - Parabellum
films.note: Part of the Memorial Day wall Booksmart ran into.

films.title: Avengers: Endgame
films.note: The market-dominating tentpole small films can't compete with for attention.

films.title: Dark Phoenix
films.note: One of the two movies Sean's 16-year-old sister was actually stoked for — Booksmart wasn't on her radar.

films.title: Men in Black: International
films.note: The other one.

films.title: Pokémon Detective Pikachu
films.note: Cited as a mid-tier movie fighting for the same airspace as Booksmart.

films.title: The Lion King
films.note: One of the handful of tentpoles that don't need the podcast's help.

films.title: Star Wars: The Rise of Skywalker
films.note: Same list of self-sustaining giants.

segments.heading: Cannes and the early Oscar field

films.title: Parasite
films.note: Bong Joon-ho's Palme d'Or winner, the first for a Korean filmmaker. Tabbed for October via Neon; the hosts expect a wide push.

films.title: Once Upon a Time in Hollywood
films.note: Tarantino's Brad Pitt/Leonardo DiCaprio/Margot Robbie film, set in 1969 LA. The Cannes press conference — a question about how little dialogue Robbie's Sharon Tate has — kicked off a two-month discourse Sean calls 'Booksmart times a million.'

films.title: A Hidden Life
films.note: Terrence Malick's WWII film, deemed a return to form. Fox Searchlight paid ~$10M — the one signal Sean trusts that it's an awards play; the reviews' 'transcendent, deeply searching' language makes him nervous it's three hours where little happens.

films.title: The Lighthouse
films.note: Robert Eggers' black-and-white, square-format two-hander with Robert Pattinson and Willem Dafoe as lighthouse keepers — period-specific, flatulent, and Amanda's favorite meme fodder of the episode.

films.title: Portrait of a Lady on Fire
films.note: Céline Sciamma's prize winner; the hosts expect a Best International Feature run.

films.title: Atlantics
films.note: Mati Diop's Cannes acquisition, also pegged as an international-feature contender.

films.title: Mektoub, My Love: Intermezzo
films.note: Abdellatif Kechiche's 212-minute follow-up that is, per the hosts, largely about butts.

films.title: Mektoub, My Love: Canto Uno
films.note: Part one, from Venice 2017.

films.title: Blue Is the Warmest Color
films.note: Kechiche's better-known film, cited as the reference point for his interest in the body.

films.title: Okja
films.note: Sean's way into Bong — he loves it.

films.title: The Host
films.note: Part of the case that Bong is plainly a genius.

films.title: Snowpiercer
films.note: Same.

films.title: Memories of Murder
films.note: Same.

films.title: Assassins
films.note: Source of the famous Antonio Banderas knuckle-biting gif — Banderas took Best Actor at Cannes for the Almodóvar film and jet-streamed into the Oscar conversation.

films.title: The Witch
films.note: Eggers' debut, invoked for The Lighthouse.

films.title: Song to Song
films.note: The Malick film Sean defends in the 'even his lesser films have chunks I dug' argument.

films.title: The Favourite
films.note: Fox Searchlight's awards pedigree — last year's rollout.

films.title: Three Billboards Outside Ebbing, Missouri
films.note: More Searchlight campaign history.

films.title: The Shape of Water
films.note: Same.

films.title: BlacKkKlansman
films.note: An Oscar movie that premiered at Cannes the prior year — you can count on a handful.

films.title: Shoplifters
films.note: The previous year's Palme d'Or winner.

films.title: Us
films.note: Sean's lone exception to 'no Oscar movie has been released yet' — a weak Best Actress year could get Lupita Nyong'o in.

segments.heading: The new political documentaries
segments.note: and where docs are landing now

films.title: Running with Beto
films.note: David Modigliani's HBO doc on Beto O'Rourke's 2017-18 Senate run; Sean saw it at South by Southwest and interviewed the director. Not appearing in theaters — the recurring problem.

films.title: Knock Down the House
films.note: The Netflix doc following AOC and three other women running for Congress in the same midterm.

films.title: The Brink
films.note: The Steve Bannon portrait — one of the few in this wave to get a theatrical release, and it hasn't done well.

films.title: Apollo 11
films.note: By far the box-office champ of the Sundance 2019 crop, and a beautiful-looking film.

films.title: The Biggest Little Farm
films.note: A family leaves the city to farm organically — dogs, pigs, moments of true terror. Amanda thought it might be HGTV; it's a real movie, and it's earned over a million dollars.

films.title: Won't You Be My Neighbor?
films.note: The Mr. Rogers doc, part of last year's 'docs are booming' theatrical story.

films.title: Free Solo
films.note: Same wave of theatrical doc events.

films.title: RBG
films.note: Same.

films.title: The Inventor: Out for Blood in Silicon Valley
films.note: This year's docs that popped landed on HBO/streaming rather than in theaters.

films.title: Fyre
films.note: Same — a two-to-nine-day conversation piece on Netflix, not a theatrical event.

films.title: The Mustang
films.note: Another Sundance 2019 title with dim box office.

films.title: Birds of Passage
films.note: Same list.

films.title: Hail Satan?
films.note: Sean rates it very good.

films.title: The Souvenir
films.note: Both hosts saw it and were floored. Set in 1980s London, it unfolds slowly and elliptically around a young film student (Honor Swinton Byrne, Tilda Swinton's daughter, in her first role) and an opaque relationship. A24 release, with Scorsese as an executive producer. Watch it knowing as little as possible.

films.title: Exhibition
films.note: Hogg's prior feature, about two married artists in mid-life — Sean's high recommendation as a way in.

films.title: The Dead Don't Die
films.note: The other Cannes premiere with Tilda Swinton, here a katana-wielding mortician — part of her year of 'bits' the naturalistic Souvenir is antithetical to.

films.title: Rocketman
films.note: The Elton John biopic with Taron Egerton and Richard Madden, premiered at Cannes. Sean floats a serious-Oscar-movie case — Egerton and John both on charm offensives — while doubting it can be a box-office hit. Full review promised Friday.

films.title: Bohemian Rhapsody
films.note: The comparison Rocketman is chasing and, Amanda argues, can't repeat — an unrepeatable $800M situation.

TV: What We Do in the Shadows
Ads: Big Little Live (Big Little Lies after-show promo)
